---
title: "DeepFormer Refactored: Public Data Reconstruction, MAT-Route Engineering, and Demo-Executable Workflow"
author: Ningyuan Zhang
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    code-copy: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    anchor-sections: true
    page-layout: article
    self-contained: true
include-in-header:
  text: |
    <style>
      :root {
        --brand: #2b6cb0;
        --brand-dark: #1e4e8c;
        --accent: #e6f0fb;
        --soft: #f8fafc;
        --border: #d9e2ec;
        --text-soft: #4a5568;
      }

      body {
        font-family: "Inter", "Segoe UI", "PingFang SC", "Microsoft YaHei", sans-serif;
        line-height: 1.75;
      }

      .quarto-title-block .title {
        color: var(--brand-dark);
        font-weight: 800;
      }

      h1, h2, h3 {
        color: var(--brand-dark);
        font-weight: 750;
      }

      h1 {
        border-bottom: 2px solid var(--border);
        padding-bottom: 0.35rem;
        margin-top: 1.5rem;
      }

      h2 {
        margin-top: 1.6rem;
      }

      .hero-box {
        background: linear-gradient(135deg, #edf5ff 0%, #f7fbff 100%);
        border: 1px solid #cfe0f5;
        border-radius: 18px;
        padding: 1.2rem 1.4rem;
        margin: 0.8rem 0 1.2rem 0;
        box-shadow: 0 10px 25px rgba(43, 108, 176, 0.08);
      }

      .hero-box h2 {
        margin-top: 0;
        margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
        border-bottom: none;
      }

      .hero-grid {
        display: grid;
        grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(230px, 1fr));
        gap: 0.8rem;
        margin-top: 1rem;
      }

      .hero-card {
        background: white;
        border: 1px solid #dbe7f3;
        border-radius: 14px;
        padding: 0.8rem 1rem;
      }

      .hero-card strong {
        color: var(--brand-dark);
      }

      pre, code {
        font-family: "JetBrains Mono", "Cascadia Code", "Consolas", monospace;
      }

      pre {
        border-radius: 12px;
        border: 1px solid #e6edf3;
      }

      .callout {
        border-radius: 14px;
      }

      table {
        font-size: 0.96rem;
      }

      blockquote {
        background: #f9fbfd;
        border-left: 4px solid var(--brand);
        padding: 0.7rem 1rem;
        border-radius: 0 10px 10px 0;
      }

      .note-box {
        background: var(--soft);
        border: 1px solid var(--border);
        border-radius: 12px;
        padding: 0.85rem 1rem;
        margin: 0.9rem 0;
      }

      .small-muted {
        color: var(--text-soft);
        font-size: 0.95rem;
      }
    </style>
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.hero-box}
## 🧬 DeepFormer Refactored

This document summarizes the final state of the **DeepFormer refactored project**, including:

- public-data reconstruction;
- HDF5-compatible MAT verification;
- MAT-route data engineering;
- demo subset generation;
- DeepFormer forward testing;
- tiny demo training and lightweight evaluation;
- the three-layer best-practice framework.

<div class="hero-grid">
  <div class="hero-card"><strong>Data status</strong><br>Publicly reconstructed and structurally verified</div>
  <div class="hero-card"><strong>Execution status</strong><br>Demo-executable MAT route completed</div>
  <div class="hero-card"><strong>Current limitation</strong><br>Not yet a full paper-level reproduction</div>
</div>
:::

# 1. Project Overview

## 1.1 Original motivation

The original DeepFormer repository relied on a legacy public-data route that was no longer reliably accessible. In addition, the original project structure was not ideal for explanation, maintenance, or step-by-step reproduction.

Therefore, the goals of this refactored project were:

1. reorganize the repository into a clearer engineering structure;
2. reconstruct a public-data route that does not depend on the expired legacy path;
3. establish an executable MAT-based workflow;
4. build a demonstration-ready train/evaluation route;
5. document the entire process in a cleaner and more teachable way.

## 1.2 Current final position

At the current stage, this project should be regarded as:

- a **presentation-ready engineering project**;
- a **demo-executable DeepFormer prototype**;
- a **public-data-reconstructed workflow**;
- **not yet** a full benchmark-grade reproduction.

::: {.callout-important}
## Current status in one sentence

DeepFormer Refactored has already evolved into a **public-data-reconstructed, MAT-route, demo-trainable, demo-evaluable engineering project**, although full paper-level reproduction remains future work.
:::

# 2. Repository Structure

## 2.1 Main directory layout

```text
deepformer-refactored/
├── config/                         # configuration files
├── data/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── raw_deepsea/                # reconstructed MAT files and demo subsets
├── docs/                           # QMD report and supporting documents
├── models/                         # model definitions
├── results/                        # lightweight logs and summaries
├── scripts/                        # testing, demo, and utility scripts
├── training/                       # original train/eval entry files
├── utils/                          # dataset loaders and helpers
├── README.md
├── USAGE.md
└── requirements.txt
```

## 2.2 Why this structure matters

This refactored layout separates:

- configuration;
- data description;
- model code;
- script-level execution;
- reusable utility code;
- result summaries;
- formal documentation.

This makes the project easier to explain in a defense setting and easier to extend in future work.

# 3. Best-Practice Framework Overview

To organize the project clearly, the work is summarized under three layers:

1. **Data Layer**
2. **Methodology Layer**
3. **Interpretability Layer**

## 3.1 Completion profile

| Layer | Current Status | Main Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Data Layer | Partially completed | public-data reconstruction, MAT verification, demo subset generation |
| Methodology Layer | Largely completed | loader, forward test, tiny training, lightweight evaluation |
| Interpretability Layer | Roadmap established | future tool-chain and attribution-to-motif workflow design |

## 3.2 Why this framework is useful

This framework makes it easier to distinguish between:

- **where the data come from**;
- **how the model is executed**;
- **how the model may later be interpreted biologically**.

It prevents the project from appearing as just a collection of scripts.

# 4. Data Layer

## 4.1 Main challenge

The original legacy DeepSEA-style public data route was no longer reliably accessible. Therefore, the project adopted a **public reconstruction workflow** rather than relying on a dead direct-download link.

## 4.2 What was completed

Using public source files and hg19, the following files were reconstructed:

- `train.mat`
- `valid.mat`
- `test.mat`

These were placed under:

- `data/raw_deepsea/train.mat`
- `data/raw_deepsea/valid.mat`
- `data/raw_deepsea/test.mat`

## 4.3 MAT verification

The reconstructed MAT files were inspected and confirmed to be **HDF5-compatible** rather than standard `scipy.io.loadmat` MATLAB files.

### Verified structure

| File | Input key | Input shape | Label key | Label shape |
|---|---|---:|---|---:|
| `train.mat` | `trainxdata` | `(4400000, 1000, 4)` | `traindata` | `(4400000, 919)` |
| `valid.mat` | `validxdata` | `(8000, 1000, 4)` | `validdata` | `(8000, 919)` |
| `test.mat` | `testxdata` | `(455024, 1000, 4)` | `testdata` | `(455024, 919)` |

The summary was saved into:

- `results/deepsea_mat_summary.txt`

## 4.4 Demo subset generation

Because the full MAT files are large, lightweight demo subsets were generated:

- `train_demo_256.npz`
- `valid_demo_256.npz`
- `test_demo_256.npz`

These subsets preserve the same basic input/label structure while being much easier to use for engineering validation and defense demonstration.

## 4.5 Current data-layer limitations

The data layer is already meaningful, but still not benchmark-grade in the strictest sense. For example, the current project does not fully implement:

- mappability control;
- GC-bias control;
- repeat/low-complexity region policy;
- blacklist-region handling;
- full leakage-control analysis.

Therefore, the data layer should currently be described as:

- publicly reconstructed;
- structurally verified;
- demo-executable;
- not yet a full benchmark-grade QC framework.

## 4.6 Supporting documents

- `docs/data_layer_best_practices.md`
- `docs/data_layer_current_status.md`

# 5. Methodology Layer

## 5.1 Current task route

At the current stage, the project is most closely aligned with a **binary-peak-style sequence modeling route**.

Reasons:

- input is sequence-shaped and one-hot-like;
- output is a 919-dimensional multilabel target;
- the demo training route uses binary cross-entropy loss;
- the route is much closer to DeepSEA/Basset-style multilabel prediction than to base-resolution profile prediction.

## 5.2 Completed methodology milestones

The following methodology-layer components were completed:

- HDF5-compatible MAT loader;
- demo NPZ route;
- DataLoader validation;
- DeepFormer constructor inspection;
- forward-pass test;
- tiny demo training;
- checkpoint saving;
- lightweight evaluation.

## 5.3 Executable chain

```text
public reconstructed data
    -> MAT verification
    -> demo subset generation
    -> PyTorch DataLoader test
    -> DeepFormer forward test
    -> tiny demo training
    -> checkpoint saving
    -> demo evaluation
```

## 5.4 Why this is already important

Even though this is not a full benchmark experiment, it already proves that:

- reconstructed public data can be connected to model code;
- the model can run on demo data;
- optimization can proceed;
- outputs and losses can be computed;
- the project is no longer only a static refactoring exercise.

## 5.5 Current methodology-layer limitations

The current route is still **demo-oriented**. It does not yet include:

- peak-vs-coverage route comparison;
- coverage-style engineering route;
- base-resolution profile route;
- full benchmark-grade training protocol.

## 5.6 Supporting documents

- `docs/methodology_layer_best_practices.md`
- `docs/methodology_layer_current_status.md`
- `docs/deepsea_mat_route.md`
- `docs/deepsea_demo_pipeline.md`
- `docs/demo_evaluation.md`

# 6. Interpretability Layer

## 6.1 Why interpretability matters

In regulatory-genomics models, prediction alone is often not the final scientific goal. A strong interpretability layer should eventually answer questions such as:

- which bases matter;
- which motif-like patterns are used;
- whether motif combinations form meaningful grammar;
- whether the learned patterns can support biological interpretation.

## 6.2 Current position

The project has **not yet completed** a full interpretability experiment. It does not yet include:

- final attribution maps;
- motif discovery results;
- grammar-level interaction analysis;
- biological validation figures.

However, the project has already completed the prerequisites needed for later interpretability work:

- runnable model;
- validated forward route;
- tiny training route;
- reloadable checkpoint.

So the interpretability layer is currently:

- conceptually defined;
- technically scaffolded;
- not yet experimentally completed.

## 6.3 Planned tool chain

The intended future tool chain includes:

- DeepLIFT
- DeepExplainer
- TF-MoDISco
- Tangermeme

## 6.4 Supporting documents

- `docs/interpretability_layer_best_practices.md`
- `docs/interpretability_layer_roadmap.md`

# 7. Step-by-Step Tutorial: How to Run This Project

This section is the practical tutorial part. It explains how to run the current demo-executable workflow.

## 7.1 Activate the environment

```bash
source "$(conda info --base)/etc/profile.d/conda.sh"
conda activate ~/ningyuan/envs/deepformer_clean
```

## 7.2 Verify the demo NPZ route

```bash
python scripts/test_deepsea_demo_npz.py
```

Expected outcome:

- dataset length is printed;
- sample shape is `(4, 1000)`;
- batch shape is `(4, 4, 1000)`;
- label shape is `(4, 919)`.

## 7.3 Inspect the DeepFormer constructor

```bash
python scripts/inspect_deepformer_signature.py
```

Expected outcome:

- the class name is printed;
- the source module is printed;
- the constructor signature is shown.

## 7.4 Run a forward-pass validation

```bash
python scripts/test_deepformer_forward.py
```

Expected outcome:

- input batch shape is printed;
- output shape is printed;
- the model successfully produces prediction tensors.

## 7.5 Run tiny demo training

```bash
python scripts/run_deepformer_demo_train.py
```

Expected outcome:

- several training-loss lines are printed;
- a validation loss is printed;
- a checkpoint is saved to `results/deepformer_demo_model.pt`.

## 7.6 Run demo evaluation

```bash
python scripts/run_deepformer_demo_eval.py
```

Expected outcome:

- average validation loss is printed;
- average binary accuracy is printed;
- output and target statistics are printed.

## 7.7 Check result files

After running the full demo route, you should expect lightweight result files such as:

- `results/deepsea_mat_summary.txt`
- `results/deepformer_demo_train_log.txt`
- `results/deepformer_demo_result_summary.txt`
- `results/deepformer_demo_eval_log.txt`
- `results/final_demo_status.txt`

# 8. Result Summaries

## 8.1 MAT verification results

A summary of MAT-file structure is stored in:

- `results/deepsea_mat_summary.txt`

## 8.2 Tiny training results

A tiny training log is stored in:

- `results/deepformer_demo_train_log.txt`

A shorter training summary is stored in:

- `results/deepformer_demo_result_summary.txt`

## 8.3 Demo evaluation results

A demo evaluation log is stored in:

- `results/deepformer_demo_eval_log.txt`

A concise final status file is stored in:

- `results/final_demo_status.txt`

## 8.4 Layer-level summaries

Additional summary files include:

- `results/methodology_layer_summary.txt`
- `results/interpretability_layer_plan.txt`

# 9. Main Supporting Documents

## 9.1 General project documents

- `README.md`
- `USAGE.md`
- `docs/final_project_status.md`
- `docs/defense_talking_points.md`

## 9.2 Data layer documents

- `docs/data_layer_best_practices.md`
- `docs/data_layer_current_status.md`

## 9.3 Methodology layer documents

- `docs/methodology_layer_best_practices.md`
- `docs/methodology_layer_current_status.md`

## 9.4 Interpretability layer documents

- `docs/interpretability_layer_best_practices.md`
- `docs/interpretability_layer_roadmap.md`

## 9.5 MAT-route and execution documents

- `docs/deepsea_data_reconstruction.md`
- `docs/runtime_gap_analysis.md`
- `docs/deepsea_mat_route.md`
- `docs/deepsea_demo_pipeline.md`
- `docs/demo_evaluation.md`

# 10. Current Limitations

The current project is still not a full paper-level reproduction.

## 10.1 Main limitations

- the original YAML runtime path still expects another FASTA / BED / TXT file family;
- the MAT route currently exists as a parallel executable route;
- there is no peak-vs-coverage paired comparison yet;
- there is no full benchmark-grade training/evaluation protocol yet;
- the interpretability layer is still roadmap-level rather than experiment-complete.

## 10.2 Why this is still acceptable

For the current stage, this limitation is acceptable because the project already demonstrates:

- public-data reconstruction from accessible sources;
- structural verification of generated dataset artifacts;
- successful model-connected execution;
- a clear engineering path for future extension.

# 11. Future Work

The most natural next steps are:

1. fully unify the MAT route with the main runtime configuration;
2. add a peak-vs-coverage comparison at the methodology layer;
3. extend to more formal benchmark-grade training and evaluation;
4. integrate attribution and motif-level interpretation tools.

# 12. Final Project Position

::: {.callout-note}
## Final one-sentence summary

This project transformed DeepFormer from a partially inaccessible legacy workflow into a publicly reconstructable, structurally verified, and demo-executable engineering project.
:::
